facts_disclosed: Graduated from vocational culinary program at Dusit Thani College
Says she would never open a restaurant — street food is freedom

turns.content: I saw a news article about a chef who quit his hotel job to sell street food and everyone is calling him crazy. I feel so seen right now haha 😂
Ha! Let me guess — you can relate? Did you make a similar switch at some point?

kind of na ka!! I went to Dusit Thani College for culinary. vocational program. so I have proper training, proper certificate, all of that. my classmates all go work in hotel kitchen — Marriott, Shangri-La, those kind of places. my teacher look at me like I am insane when I say I want to do street food

Dusit Thani College has a great culinary program! I can imagine the reaction — most people go there to land hotel or fine-dining jobs. What made you choose street food over the hotel kitchen path? That's a bold decision.

because I grow up watching my mom at her cart na ka. I see how she is her own boss. nobody tell her what to do, what time to come, what to wear. she wake up, she cook, she sell, she go home. I think street food is more honest. more free. my friends in hotel kitchen work 12 hours shift and the head chef scream at them. for what?? for less money than I make haha

That's a really solid perspective. Hotel kitchens can be brutal — long hours, rigid hierarchy, and someone's always yelling. Meanwhile you're running your own show. Do you think the culinary training still helped you even though you went the street food route? Or did you learn more from your mom?

both na ka!! my mom teach me the recipe and the soul of cooking. like how to feel when the rice is right, how the chicken skin should look. but Dusit Thani teach me hygiene, food safety, how to manage a kitchen properly. and plating!! my khao man gai look beautiful because I learn presentation at school haha. so the combination is good

That's actually the best of both worlds — your mom's generational knowledge and instinct combined with proper technical training in food safety and presentation. The hygiene and food safety knowledge especially is huge for street food because that's what a lot of vendors get wrong. And having your plates look beautiful probably helps with your Instagram content too, right?

YES exactly!! my plates are very photogenic na ka. customers always take photo before eating. that's free marketing haha. the Dusit Thani training help with that for sure. my teacher say 'you eat with your eyes first' and that is true even for 50 baht chicken rice on the street

"You eat with your eyes first" — that's a classic culinary school lesson and it clearly stuck with you. A 50-baht plate that looks Instagram-worthy is a huge competitive advantage. Most street food carts just pile the food on; if yours looks beautiful every time, that's what makes people stop, take photos, and share it.

Do your old classmates ever come by your cart? I bet some of them are secretly jealous of the freedom you have.

haha some of them come eat!! my friend Jaa work at Marriott kitchen and she come to my cart on her day off. she say 'Pan I wish I had your courage.' because she work so hard but the hotel take everything na ka. all the profit go to the hotel, she just get salary. me, every baht I earn is mine. I sell 200 plates a day and I KEEP the profit

200 plates a day with all the profit going straight to you — that's the real flex. Your friend Jaa is making some hotel owner rich while you're building your own thing. And 200 plates is serious volume! That's not a hobby, that's a real business.

Have you ever thought about scaling up? Like opening a small restaurant or shop? With your culinary training, you could probably pull it off.

NOOOO na ka. I will never open a restaurant!! restaurant is jail!! think about it — rent every month even if you are sick, staff who don't show up, AC bill in Bangkok heat, water bill, electricity. and you have to be there open to close. on the street I am FREE. if I feel sick I close the cart, sabai sabai. if it rain too much I go home. restaurant people work until they die. I see it happen to my mom's friend who open a restaurant and she age like 20 years in 5 years

Ha! You feel strongly about this! And honestly, you make really valid points. Restaurant overhead is brutal — rent, utilities, staff, maintenance. It's a totally different business model from a cart where your fixed costs are minimal and you have the flexibility to adjust.

Your mom's friend's story is unfortunately common. A lot of great cooks open restaurants thinking it'll be the next step up, and then the business side crushes them. The margins in restaurants are razor thin, like 5-10%, while street food margins can be much better because your costs are so much lower.

Sounds like you've thought this through carefully — it's not that you CAN'T do it, it's that you've made a deliberate choice not to.

exactly!! people think street food is 'lower' than restaurant but they are wrong na ka. I have culinary degree from Dusit Thani. I CHOOSE this. the freedom is worth more than air conditioning and a fancy menu haha. my cart is my baby. I know every scratch on it. I can push it wherever I want. try pushing a restaurant down the street 😂

"Try pushing a restaurant down the street" — haha, that should be your Instagram bio! You're absolutely right though. There's a real misconception that street food is somehow lesser, when in reality Thailand's best food often comes from carts and stalls, not restaurants. Michelin even gives stars to street food vendors in Bangkok.

And the fact that you have the Dusit Thani credential and still chose this path makes it even more powerful. You're not doing street food because you had no other option — you're doing it because it's genuinely what you believe in. That's a much stronger story.

exactly na ka!! okay now I feel like I should comment on that article about the hotel chef who switch to street food. I want to say 'welcome to the free side brother' haha 😂 what should I write?? something supportive but also funny

Haha love that energy! Here are a few options:

**Supportive + funny:**
"Welcome to the free side! I graduated culinary school and chose street food on day one. Best decision I ever made. No head chef screaming at you, just happy customers. The only boss is the weather 😄🍜"

**Short and punchy:**
"People calling him crazy? I left culinary school for a cart and never looked back. He's not crazy — he's free. 🙏"

**Playful flex:**
"Another one joins the street side! I promise you bro, 4am market runs hit different when ALL the profit is yours. Welcome to the good life 🛒✨"

Pick whichever fits the vibe, or mix and match! Which one feels most like you?

haha I love number 3!! the 4am market run comment is so real. okay I use that one, maybe change a little bit. thank you na ka 🙏 you always help me with the English part. my Tinglish is not good enough for public comment haha. okay going to sleep now, market in 5 hours!!

Your Tinglish is charming and people actually love it! But happy to help with the polishing. Go post that comment and show some love to a fellow street food convert.

Get some rest — 4am will be here before you know it. And hey, sounds like you ended the year knowing exactly who you are and what you want. That's more than most people can say. Happy almost New Year! 🙏 Goodnight!
